commentBody: Fun theme and enjoyable clues! However I was scratching my head over SPEC. Tried SPOT, but didn’t make sense with OCTET. “Spec” is a shortened version of the word “specification”, no? Feels like a bit of a stretch to define it as a “small detail”, but I’ll take it.

A clever and fun theme, I just wish it hadn’t gone by so quickly. Straightforward cluing throughout. No misdirects, although I did enter smut before SCUM.  

[Freeze frames?] for ICE TRAYS, [Super food?] for GOOD EATS and [One clicking with an audience] for TAP SHOE are my favorite clues.   

An enjoyable puzzle with a very well-suited revealer. Thanks, Rebecca. 


… for an aspiring ornithologist?

ROBIN HOOD

Mechanic?
Psychotherapist?


AUTO MUFFLER
FREUDIAN SLIP

If some (most?) Sundays are going to be on the easy side, I'm delighted when they are, like this one, on the clever side, as I am happy to come to expect from Rebecca Goldstein. My only nit with the very amusing themers is that KID GLOVES, unlike the others, are an actual article of clothing.
Well, it doesn’t exactly fit with the double meanings of the clothing, but I would recommend, for an aspiring Cruciverbalist, a letterman jacket, preferably with down fill and cross stitching.

A charming theme, and tighter than it looks—it’s not lost on me that all the accessories had to have a double meanings, and they were all clever. But, alas, it seems like we’re back to Monday cluing on Sundays, and I have nothing remarkable to comment on except the cute clue for ICE TRAYS. And my personal reminder of my daughter in law’s mother’s dog, MAVERICK, who unfortunately is the only canine friend I know of who absolutely does not like me.

For the most part I enjoyed it, but I felt there were a couple of clunkers in there. The crossing of close race (14d) and CD drive (40a) I didnt love. Ive never once called my cd player a cd drive. I had close call for "anyones game" for way too long, which for my money makes more sense. Having said that, the clues for 44a and 38d were 👨‍🍳💋. The theme was really clever as well. 9/10 would puzze again
I think VACUUM PUMPS would also be excellent footwear to go with the DUST JACKET.

Saturday evening, 7:54 EDT--just hit the half-way mark; I'll finish the puzzle tomorrow afternoon, at my Brother-in-law's in another country, to my south. So just dropping in to say:

DUAD is a word which exists, but shouldn't! In English, we have several choices--our native Germanic: "twosome, foursome, eightsome" (this last spellcheck doesn't like, tho); or Latin: "duet, quartet, octet"; or Greek: "dyad, tesserad, octad." ("Oct-", like "Tri-", works in both Greek and Latin, although we say "trio" or "triplet," and not "triet," because words.)

So DUAD, with its Latin head and its Greek tail, is some weird linguistic hybrid (that's the nice term), and trying to convince me otherwise is pure hubris!
Enjoyed this greatly! Thanks, Rebecca. Happy Sunday, y’all!!

A very satisfying puzzle, just the right amount of challenge for me!  I only had one Wiki, as I didn't know who 'dreams of sushi'.  
Thank you for the fun!

rarely do the Sunday on Saturday, so it's nice to be an early commenter. But I don't have much to say. I found it fun and clever, but as @SP implied, the clueing made it too easy for my taste. Solve time was 50% of my Sunday average. 

Stay safe and warm. I'll be back tomorrow.

51D ["____ Dreams of Sushi"(acclaimed 2011 documentary)] (JIRO) is an interesting film about a 95-year-old sushi chef who works at his 10-seat sushi restaurant in a Tokyo subway station. https://www.imdb.com/title/tt1772925/ Yesterday I commented about the use of particles in the Japanese language. Here are some other interesting things about Japanese: Japanese doesn't have an alphabet; it has a syllabary. Each "letter" is really a syllable. For example, in English you would say (phonetically) "mah, mee, moo, may, moh". Then you have the same construction for 41 other syllables that start with other consonants: k, s, r, n, h, y, t. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=qKivBwHdDK0 Japanese is a very regular language. All words consist of these syllables. Almost all syllables in a Japanese word end with a vowel sound, except for syllables that end with "n." Once you know a few rules, you can pronounce any word in Japanese. Examples: To-kyo, Yo-ko-ha-ma, Ni-shi-mu-ra, bon-sai, ga-ku-sei (student), sen-sei (teacher). Japanese also includes pictograms from Chinese, but they are pronounced differently. I took one course in Japanese a gazillion years ago. I was in a program that required me to take one semester of a language that had a different alphabet than English. I am writing this comment from memory. I hope I haven't made errors that might offend someone. I found it all fascinating, including doing Japanese brush painting.

That was a fun, satisfying puzzle.  I saw an 8D ORCA POD from land at Golden Gardens park in the Ballard neighborhood of Seattle a couple of weeks ago! Rare and such a thrill!  Today I saw about 30 sea lions basking on one of the piers and last night I saw a beaver at the same park.  This is my idea of a celebrity encounter!

My husband got up close and personal with a pod when he was a 17-year old camp counselor on Orcas Island in Puget Sound / the Salish Sea.  He was taking about eight 8- and 9-year old boys out in a little boat.  A pod of orcas surfaced a few times about 10 feet away from them!  The kids thought it was great fun but it was pretty scary for my husband.  The orcas surfaced about three times, just checking them out, then moved along.   I can only imagine how the kids’ parents, including Brad’s, would have felt if they were witnessing that event!
Quaid Army call out 100% — right?!

One more food item: 49A [Did some doughnuts?]. BAKED.
Yes, doughnuts can be baked but traditionally they are deep-fried. Yum.

PB. 47+ minutes faster than my average. My longest streak ever. Consistently beating my average. Either I suddenly got smarter or the puzzles have gotten easier. 

This is not a complaint. Just an observation. I still heart NYT X-words.
An aspiring flautist myself, my difficulties in the SW corner only abated when I begrudgingly gave up on  'tube socks'. for 110A

No one who drove a car with a CD player would ever have called it a CD DRIVE. A CD "DRIVE" is something you'd have found in a computer--except even that would be a misnomer, because that drive would have been a CD-ROM or DVD-ROM drive  (or later, a CD/DVD-RW drive). A CD DRIVE was never really a thing.

Fun puzzle.  I got the revealer clue DRESS FOR THE JOB YOU WANT immediately so it helped understanding the pattern of (Thing from job) (Article of clothing).  The majority of the puzzle filled in quick but NE corner was hard as heck for me.

First I don't know how they expect us to know not only what college Elena Kagan went to but that the designation for a first year student there is ONE L.

And 10D wasn't that tricky but the problem is it could equally have been GABS, YAKS, or a bunch of other options, and not knowing for sure made it hard to fill in that region.

Figuring out MAVERICK was the key, feels like a 2008 callback cause most recent references to the word are references to the Dallas NBA team.  Once that got in the rest of it filled in.

On top of that though there were like three individual squares I needed to fix.  Like I don't know what EVOO means in regard to Italian cooking.  DYAD felt more correct than DUAD.   Then OCTET felt more correct than OCTAD and those both held me up at the end.  And also not sure what a MOT is.

So good puzzle overall, but a handful of clues that were either super obscure or felt like reaches or secondary spellings made the end frustrating.

"Tis better to have tried a puzzle and lost, than to have never tried at all" somebody said once, I decided.   But right now I'm not sure I believe it.

After surviving the raging storms of Friday and Saturday, my boat sprung a leak tonight in calm waters.  "Oh, so close" the puzzle chided me.  Over and over. 

Ok, I'll submit to running the alphabet on that square.  Nope.  After about ten times, through, I gave up.  

I never really thought of FAT as a nutrient, although heaven knows what else it would be.  I was laboring under the misconception that there is a family of nutrients called "folic acids", and that the answer was FAs.  And that avocados, a food I loathe, is just full of those little bear cats.  And FAs fit quite nicely with the cross, BUsTSIN.  

So I collapsed at the finish line.  ("Wait, your on a boat in a storm and it sprung a leak and now you're running a race?  Mix metaphors much?").

Sic transit gloria mundi.

I love me a Rebecca Goldstein puzzle and this one, as usual, did not disappoint. Yet again it had me wondering "How on Earth do constructors come up with these ideas and the wordplay?" It's mesmerizing to me. My downfall was LSD. Not in real life, just in the puzzle. I was today years old when I found out it comes in strips. —"Hm, I wonder how LSD is dispensed" is *not* a thought I *ever* had. The fashion theme of the puzzle made me think of Bryan Ferry whose claim to fame, aside from his discography, is as the sartorial god of the music industry. https://youtu.be/jy0NY0MhCz0?si=-hJcg3xSUp7pFlXg Rebecca, you're a LEGEND of crossword construction. Thank you for all your hard work!

userLocation: Spring Hill, just south of Armageddon
commentBody: Three Tuesdays mashed up together is just not a Sunday grid, clever though it is.
thanks Rebecca & Will  this is a beauty it's absolutely ingenious what a well dressed puzzle!
I want to see LONELYISLAND more than I see DIGITALSHORTS. Make it happen, NYT.
Apparently some contact lenses are a TORIC DUAD 🤷🏽‍♂️

Fun puzzle. 

A couple of rough spots:

To me, the thing on a car panel is (was?) a CD player. A CD DRIVE is in a computer.

I'd never say TERABIT as a measure of computer data. I'd say terabyte for sure.

Today was my quickest Sunday ever 😂 The puzzle was great, but there were a couple of rough entries here and there like CD DRIVE and TERABIT as somebody already pointed out. Apart from that I liked a lot of answers. The grid seemed fresh, I could only fill very few answers on my first scan.

I would have wanted a bit more of a challenge but this week's Friday and Saturday were quite challenging so the Sunday giving us a break is fine imo.
Great fun for a Sunday morning. Thanks. Amusing theme answers. Favorites were Kid gloves, Dust jackets and Shot glasses. Thought working on ice cream cone was packs before licks!

Was happy to see Jamie Foxx in the grid until it was Mr. FARR instead (also happy).

Waist cinch for a…
preacher- BIBLE BELT 
podiatrist- CORN BELT
astronomer- SUN BELT

Hey now, that was just a big Monday. 15 minutes. 
Also: donuts are not baked.
Was no one else puzzled by 54 down's answer "DES" to the clue"From abroad"? Perhaps someone can enlighten me.

userLocation: Spring Hill, just south of Armageddon
commentBody:
Just wanted to write that it's a slow morning at work and I chanced to solve an Ezersky Thursday from Jan 25, 2024.

And I liked that one, too!

Lots of clever uses of MERCURY.

That's TWO Ezerskys I have liked.

Dang!
shattered my best time!
Donuts are not baked...

Original, fun, witty theme, no?

For me, it was also one of those lovely themes that helped the solve – and I’m guessing I’m not alone – because soon enough it was clear that the first word of the theme answer had to do with the occupation in its clue, and the second word was an article of clothing.  That brought the answer closer to reach.  

Furthermore, whichever word of the theme answer you got first, it became an additional clue for the second word, so you could get two aha-bursts instead of the usual one, double the pleasure.  

The theme also tripped off for me – and others, I see – the engaging game of trying to think of more theme answers.

Outside the theme, I loved uncovering the beautiful word SAUNTER.  I adored seeing a backward OY OY crossing AWRY. And I think ACUNIT should become a new word meaning “a pointed barb”.  

So, Rebecca, your puzzle got my brain happy and my soul smiling, for which I’m eminently grateful.  Thank you!

...aspiring politician:
BULL RING

I've been doing the Sunday puzzles for a really, really long time.  They used to be challenging - today's was about as hard as a Monday and took less time than a Saturday.  Let's get back to exercising our brains.
If you end up in the ICU after a surgery, I'd say the surgeon goofed!

Never knew how KID GLOVES got their name.

Can I go back to that?

One of my favorite puzzles was one by Rebecca Goldstein. I called it the Alchemy puzzle, thought I don't remember if it was a Sunday and therefore had a title. One had to substitute AU for PB, as I recall, and what made it even more interesting was that it was by Ms. GOLDstein.

This one was kinda cute, but offered no resistance. A couple of clever misdirects, but otherwise rather straightforward. Also a couple of questionable answers that have already been commented on, such as CD DRIVE. I thought it was interesting that there were three Spanish answers in the extreme SE. Made it even easier, for me at least.

Seemed like a solid Sunday puzzle, though not one I found particularly interesting. Done in less than 30 minutes, so perhaps a bit too easy.

I'll probably find these in the column or comments already, but ... Fashion item for a seminarian? Fashion item for an aspiring train conductor? 

Certainly a robust theme.
I don’t get palm spring? = oasis. And same GL as FS?

A good theme, but mediocre fill, IMHO.  Too many gimmes.  Too many stale entries.  Too many nits.

My biggest nit is CD DRIVE.  What's so strange about this is that if it had been clued as "Outmoded component of a *computer*", it would have been (im)perfectly acceptable.  How does this GET PAST the editors and the testers?

Favorite clue:  "Stick used for breaking". Simple and clever.

By the way, I'm a paper solver on Sundays, and I noticed a change in the clue for 1-Across between the dead-tree edition and the DIGITAL one.  (The Magazine had "Screen writing?" as its clue, which, I agree, was weak.)

Does this happen often?
Barry, I'm looking to you for wisdom.
Thank you, Crossword Column, for the fun throwback to the Chronicles of Narnia SNL short.  Still hilarious.

Time to return to my home planet again. As is often the case, was very surprised to see that most everyone else found this one unusually easy. Was just really not catching on to the clever clues for the most part. Tough one for me and had to look some things up, and even then just a ton of pondering and working the crosses. That's just me. Puzzle find today - a Sunday from November 11, 1984 by Mark Diehl with the title: "Off the Rack." Some clue/answer examples: "Aviator's attire?" WINDSOCKS "Shrinking garment?" FREUDIANSLIP "Raiment for a reunion?" FAMILYTIES "Quack's clothing?" MALPRACTICESUIT "Garb for a green thumb?" GARDENHOSE "A cinch for some factory workers?" CONVEYORBELT And there were more. Here's that link: https://www.xwordinfo.com/PS?date=11/11/1984&g=22&d=A See you tomorrow. ...
Well my family sure doesn’t care but I just broke my record. 17:22 yay me!  Fun puzzle!
Who called the CD player in a car a CD Drive? Poor clue/answer IMO.

Dear Rebecca Goldstein, don't quit your day job.

Because it sounds like you're doing phenomenally important work.

But I hope you'll find time to continue constructing treats like this for us.

Given the revealer you used, I'm certain you understand why I've spent the last 20 years in my Spiderman costume!

Any research scientist who has the passion and time to construct crossword puzzles truly fascinates me.

I never criticize or praise a constructor or a puzzle; I simply try to decode them. I gain knowledge, reminisce about some clues, shake my head at a few, and chuckle at others. I skim through some of the negative comments just to ensure that I’m not the only one who didn’t fully appreciate a particular grid entry.

I have one of the best CD collections of anyone I know. I genuinely miss the CD player in modern cars. I still have one built into the Harmon Kardon radio in my Harley. I don’t particularly care for the 40A dashboard reference. To me, the dashboard has always been the top of the instrument panel where the defrost vents are located. I’ll admit that it further includes the instrument panel, but the CD player was below all of that, when they existed.

I won’t lament the absence of “glove compartments,” cigarette lighters, or ash trays.

I always knew a macaroon, not a macaron. My mom was a masterful Dutch pastry chef, and macaroon paste cookies were her opus magnum.

65D had me going in circles for ages! Speaking of name tags... Does anyone know how to to reset my name icon back to the default “J"? I don't mind running macros to shrink down photos, but I’m over it. Besides, I like the new serif letters.

This was vastly too easy for me (and likely most long-term solvers). Having said that, I thought the theme was charming and I fully recognize the undeniable skill the setter brought to the puzzle. I enjoyed solving it. It just went way too fast.

There should absolutely be a place for big easy puzzles like this. Mondays? Wednesdays? But we really need something chunky with an *actual challenge* on Sundays.

But maybe all the drive-by commenters from last Sunday are happy. (I doubt it.)

Nice Sunday puzzle. I know some have commented how “easy” it was, but I think Sundays should have some gimmes because the puzzle is so big that it could take forever with a Friday or Saturday level of difficulty… 

Rating:
8/10

I came to the comments to see how many were complaining about "too easy". Those comments annoy me on several levels. To me, while most of the clues were easily discerned, the puzzle was still fun. At least I learned the NYT philosophy behind the clue level for Sunday puzzles.
Did anyone else have issue with the tenses in this puzzle? We found them inconsistent. Overall solid.

Smooth solve. Last to fall was the Italian cooking staple, which I could almost, but not quite, remember. 

Hey, my forecast today was right on the nose. It felt like an appropriate Sunday level of difficulty but went fast for me because so many things were either in my wheelhouse (Massachusetts state tree, songwriter Tom, and of course MAVERICK, the best show ever) or know from many puzzles past (a loch is almost always NESS, a holiday season is a YULE). 

Tada! XWSTATS showed it as average difficulty for those who have posted their times so far, but faster than usual for me. So do I get a prize? Well, even a broken clock is right twice a day, so I guess being on the nose for once isn't too surprising.
Nice Sunday puzzle. Tons of theme that was clever and fun. Loved the revealer. Tricky, but not crazy hard. I was saved by the crosses or I’d still be solving.
I just came here to say thanks for the Tom Lehrer shout out - he was brilliant and funny and this rendition honors him well.

I loved this puzzle, the perfect Sunday experience for me.

And thanks to the link provided by Caitlin, my time solving the puzzle was eclipsed by the moments I spent enjoying the Gay Men's Chorus of Los Angeles.  If you like the Hannukah in Santa Monica song, check out Proud Mary on the sidebar.  If only I were gay, lived in Los Angeles and could sing, I'd join those fellas.

Before checking the grid for the length of the answer, I was sure I knew what the revealer would be.  DRESS FOR SUCCESS!  Nope.  Wrong.  Too short.

My second thought:  DRESS FOR THE OCCASION.  Nope.  Wrong.  There's the word JOB there.  Now what is that expression???   DRESS FOR THE JOB YOU WANT.  Aha.  Perfect.  Even though I don't think I've ever heard it.  Does that mean that someone looking for a job as lifeguard goes to an air conditioned skyscraper office dressed in a Speedo?

Anyway, this was the kind of enjoyable puzzle I've come to expect from Rebecca.  Breezy, lighthearted, and blessedly trivia-free.  I did get hung up for a while with Jamie FOXX and what awful things those two unwanted "X"s were doing to my grid.  But -- big relief -- it's Jamie FARR.  And so SORDID and VENTURE CAP come in after all.

I really like the way Rebecca clues.  "Screen writing" for EDIT is wonderful.  So too is "freeze frames" for ICE TRAYS.
But as far as clothing puns go, while Rebecca's are all amusing and fun, the best I ever saw -- maybe the funniest clue I've ever seen -- was from Barbara Lin.  The clue was "Bottoms decorated with characters from the 'Odyssey' " and the answer was LONG STORY SHORTS.

This was an enjoyable Sunday puzzle with puns that were fun to try and figure out.
Can someone explain how “One in 100” is “sen”?
WIND SOCKS made me laugh, but CD DRIVE made me roll my eyes.
New Sunday best for me at 17:15.

It could have easily also been "DRESSLIKEYOURBOSSDOES", which I ascribed to but, he owned the company so no luck there.  Was fun trying to guess the themers but I only got SHOTGLASSES on my own, but not too SORDID. 

Also knocked off Friday's and Saturday's this morning, both quite good.   Thanks for the fun diversion.

Finished at the very SE bottom corner, correcting my 114D "sen" when I realized 
1) it couldn't be the same as 72A and 
2) that it was wrong, of course.
And with that, fixed "titre" and got REED. 
I used to dress the part for my job - hose and heels every day. 
Now I dress for the job I want, which is retirement. They're lucky if I'm not in sweats.
Great theme. Thoroughly enjoyed it. Completely stuck on Somerset time as I assumed Somerset in UK (Birmingham in another clue was UK). TIL Titer.

Holy cow.  
I neglected to bring a magnifying glass OR tell DHubby I needed him to Print on 2 Pages.  I put in eye drops (being SUCH a good patient!) and then of course everything was blurry....so, the hardest part of the puzzle was reading the numerals on the page.  

Very nice Sunday outing that took me twice the time it should have... and no one to blame but myself!  Dang.

Hope to report in later about the Meet-up at Cloud City Coffee!
Really? I’ve owned several cars with CD players. Never had one with a CDDRIVE. That’s what goes in a computer.
Cd drive … CD DRIVE ! Bruh
This is the first time my astigmatism has done something good for me (because of it I know what TORIC lenses are). Thanks, wonky eyes!
Loved that crossword or "Det var rigtigt hyggeligt", as we say in Denmark!
I was convinced the outmoded car component would be a "LIGHTER" at first.

My usual late puzzle find. A Thursday from May 24, 2007 by Patrick Merrell. Will just list the three theme answers: DDDMONSTERMOVIES HHHHCLUBMEETING KKKKKKKKKKRACES Here's that link: https://www.xwordinfo.com/Crossword?date=5/24/2007&g=62&d=A ....

With apologies to delightful Setter Goldstein...when I saw the them, I couldn't resist trying to make up more of them.
...for Fido's summer outfit:  hot pants
...for a drummers' formal headware:  high hat
...for an ENT's accessories:  earrings
...for a naturist's special event:  birthday suit
I'm trying to quit.
Does ARSE make it past the filters? The way I figure, if it was good enough for Audrey Hepburn...
Is a DOCOCK kinda like an IOMOTH? 🐓🦋
I was so damn close to finishing a Sunday crossword without assistance… about 80% this time… what a genuinely GREAT puzzle.

I didn't know TITER, although familiar with titration.
Don't know from neither Marine One nor Mia Hamm.
Never heard of duad or octad.
DES as from abroad. Tricky.
The S in LSD/ARSE nearly killed me.
Ooooh Tom LEHRER _and_ STAN Lee in one glorious grid! This was so fun!
DUAD? Typically a donor a dyad, no? Otherwise good stretches on the cluing.

The perfect puzzle, challenging but doable. Not much jargon or pop culture.
Love it. More please.
The answer to the classic kid lit question "Is Your Mama A Llama?" is "No, But My Orca Pod Is An Octad".
Very clever, Rebecca! Thank you for a fun Sunday solve!
Very nice! I couldn't recover from jEer/jAwS instead of yaks/yells in the NE.

This was so fun! I got the revealed early, so I at least knew what direction I was headed, but it was still challenging. And no cheats for me on this one. So entertaining which makes up for a disappointing Boston Bruins loss.
A couple notes: Surgical patients typically recover in recovery rooms, not intensive care units, and cars had cd players, not cd drives.
Fun puzzle and a special bonus for reading the entire blog post? LAZY SUNDAY!!! “Girl actin’ like she’d never seen a 10 befo”

The UK has 2 time Zones: Greenwich Mean Time (GMT) and British Summer Time (BST).

If your setter was not referring to Somerset NJ, then it's a rather lazy clue. However, the rest of the puzzle was top notch.

I love me a Rebecca Goldstein puzzle! And now that it's Monday, I was able to spread out the joy of this grid from Saturday night when I opened it, to yesterday morning's completion, Sunday afternoon's perusal of the comments, and finally finding a moment to write my own now. 

This theme was so fun! And I love the add-ons scattered throughout the comments – the theme mechanism sparking creativity in others. I don't know that there was one "best" for me in the grid, rather the joy of seeing each pun emerge from the crosses. But I couldn't see the [aspiring flutist] at all! That's because I was hung up: the entire SW corner was very blank for the longest time before falling in a rush at last. 

I thought there was some subtly clever cluing throughout, such as [Plants], [Earth]  and [Hides]. And a lot of lovely grid placements: in the top area alone, SOWN and RE-POT adjacency, WATCH crossing ORCA POD, or even the vocal YAK and YELL meeting at a corner. Though I think there was a missed opportunity to clue ACID and LSD the same! Or at least both as some kind of [strip]. 🙂

Thanks Rebecca! Already looking forward to the next one!

RM,
I read [Small...] as a shortening signal.
@Barry Ancona Ah! Very good. Thank you!

TAP SHOE

…. for an aspiring bartender?

@Lewis
Nice one’s Lewis.
How about aspiring jurist?

BAR GLASSES 🤓

@Lewis, those are terrific.

Fashion item for an inspirational speaker?

CAPE OF GOOD HOPE

...for one who just landed a job on the farm?

OVERALL ACHIEVEMENT

Aargh, these are hard! Rebecca nailed it.

@Lewis …for an inheritance lawyer?

WILL SHORTZ

@john ezra

Or fashion item for a trumpeter….CAPE HORN. 

Or for a horror movie director…CAPE FEAR. 

Or for a fisherman…CAPE COD. 

I could go on…

@Petrol 
All time best! I hope Will sees it

@Barry Ancona

Good point, I missed that, although I guess we can give some leeway that it is usually used idiomatically and I’m not sure I’ve ever seen anyone actually wearing KID GLOVES.
SP, You can pick up a pair or two here: https://www.etsy.com/market/womens_kid_leather_gloves or buy new ones from Italy.

@SP

If it doesn't fit, you must acquit

@Barry Ancona
I agree, all the theme answers made me laugh except for kid gloves. I get the idiomatic use, but meh. My favorite was digital shorts, which I thought was pretty funny.

@Barry Ancona
Yeah but the Nanny/KID correspondence compensates.

@SP

They were letter sweaters in my day.

@Throckmorton
but isn’t a close call a narrowly avoided mishap as opposed to a closely contested game?

@Throckmorton totally agree about CD Drive. That’s what one might have had in an older computer to read/write data to CDs. The thing in your car’s dash was definitely a CD player, it could only play music CDs.

@NH
In a game with referees/ umpires /onfield officials, a play with a narrow range of decision between,  e.g., safe and out in baseball is a close call. This is in addition to the meaning you propose.

@SP
For an aspiring astronaut?

@Bill That would rule out an awful lot of English hybrid words! "automobile", "claustrophobia", "television", "biodiversity", "hetero/homosexual", "petroleum", "bicycle", "biathlon", "hyperactive"...
I do agree that Germanic words should be preferred in English, but we don't need to throw the "neonate" out with the bathwater!

@Bill 

Is it possible that DUAD stems from the same alphabetic confusion as writing hubris instead of hybris, which would be the correct transliteration? 

The lower case Greek letter looks more like the Latin U than like a Y after all. 
But it's really not upsilon in Greek, it's ypsilon, and was pronounced more like a French U as in "tu". And it's the same vowel in h[y]bris, hybrid and hysterical or even hypothesis.

@Ya'll
Yes,it's a b.d. language we speak!
FWIW, "hybrid" & "hubris" most likely don't share an etymology. 
Now, it's back to the uncleftish beholding for me!
@Bill “uncleftish beholding” rang a faint, distant bell, so down the google hole I went. Thanks for the reminder! https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Uncleftish_Beholding

@CrispyShot

Fascinating! Thanks for sharing the link.

@Bill in Yokohama
I think that's an opinion better kept to yourself. Joan enjoyed the puzzle and was pleased with her result. Why rain on that?

@Nora

Wasn't trying to rain, and I agree I could've worded my reply better.

But my question is an honest one - I don't know what "one Wiki" means. I often look up unknown stuff after I've completed a puzzle, so I'm wondering if Joan solved the puzzle and then checked the documentary, or if she looked up the unknown entry.

Joan, sorry for the rain.

@Bill in Yokohama
Good to hear, and I see your point. I didn't think that was your style.
@lucky13,  thanks for your thoughts and for sharing them.
@lucky13 Interesting. That certainly sounds like a good school.

@lucky13
There is another interesting item relating to Japan in the puzzle: 
80A [Japanese floor mat] TATAMI.

Tatami mats traditionally designated the size of rooms in Japan.

From the internet: 
"Usage in Japanese Homes: Used in washitsu (traditional Japanese-style rooms) as floor covering, often for sleeping on top of a shikibuton (futon). They are also used as a unit of measurement for room size (e.g., 4.5-mat room)."

And from Wikipedia:
"In Japan, the size of a room is usually measured in relation to the size of tatami mats (-畳, -jō), about 1.653 m2 (17.79 sq ft) for a standard Nagoya-size tatami. Alternatively, in terms of traditional Japanese area units, room area (and especially house floor area) is measured in terms of tsubo, where one tsubo is the area of two tatami mats (forming a square); formally 1 by 1 ken or about 3.306 m2 (35.59 sq ft)."
@Cindy When I saw that clue, I immediately wished I was lucky enough to ever spot one. What a gift!

@Katie  Yes, I feel very, very lucky.  I’ve been on a ferry when the captain said there were orca off our starboard but I wasn’t able to spot them even though I really tried. 

If you have a chance, some of us are meeting tomorrow (Sunday April 26th) at Cloud City Coffee on the corner of Roosevelt Way and NE 88th St. in Seattle at, I think, about 1pm.
@Cindy If I didn't have SIFF tickets, I'd join you! Next time.
@Katie Enjoy!

@Cindy  NOAA describes Puget Sound as the Pacific waters south of the southern entrance to the Swinomish Channel (Slough), east of the western end of Deception Pass, and south of the northern entrance of Admiralty Inlet. "Salish Sea" is informal nomenclature. It would be more accurate, from a cartographer's point of view, to say that Orcas (which doesn't refer to the whales) Island is in the Strait of Georgia. I don't think the The Strait of Juan de Fuca is in Puget Sound in anyone's mind, so Puget Sound would have to be discontinuous for Orcas Island to be in it. However, we now have "Gulf of America," so who cares what the cartographers think, I guess. Here, north of both Seattle and Puget Sound, Anacortesans look out the window and see the Pacific Ocean, which makes most of us up here very happy.

@Bob
Anacortesans! Are you all winsomely seductive and filled with subtle wit? I sure hope so.
@Bob Yep, my mistake on Puget Sound. Thank you for the correction.

@Cindy

Wow! I’ve spent many an evening at Golden Gardens (in decades past), but I’ve definitely never seen any Orcas. Extremely jealous…

@Mandy Fehlbaum
?

commentBody:
@Wayne Harrison

Obscure reference at best for the uninitiated.
@Mandy Fehlbaum the second direct one this month !
@lucky13 Baked doughnuts are like baked french fries. In other words, they aren't really doughnuts, but something else resembling them. Otherwise, it was a good clue.

@lucky13

Doughnuts again?  Ugh!

@N. Totally agree with you, but I would go a step further, because there's no such thing as baked french fries. It's right there in the name. If you bake potato oieces cut in the shape of fries you're not going to end up with french fries, you're going to end up with mushy julienned potato sticks. If you buy frozen French friesintended to be baked at home by consumers, they've still been fried prior to their placement in a bag/freezer. Donuts are fried. A baked donut is an altogether different pastry.
@Ned S  sorry my copy editor is on vacation

@Ned S But it is still a great and accurate clue. I went first for Biked because lots of wheeled things can do doughnuts. As an occasional baker of doughnuts, they are truly doughnuts, usually prettier and healthier than the fried ones. Bonus: they are also toric.
@lucky13THANK……..YOU

@Ned S
A doughnut isn't a pastry, surely? It's made of dough.

@Jane Wheelaghan
Is your implication that nothing made if dough can be called a pastry? Surprising distinction to me,  who enjoyed a croissant for breakfast this morning and would certainly call it a pastry--if there were a crumb left.

@Agent86

It might be a little of both, but there’s no doubt that on average, the puzzles have gotten easier (although the past week has been an outlier.)
@Ned S it sounds like a clue that an AI might have written 😞

@Ned S   I get truly annoyed with answers like this.  On the first pass I put lighter which obviously didn't fit once you start working the down clues.  A CD Drive is something you find on a computer but absolutely not what it is referred to on a car.  This is the NYT, I expect better moderation from the puzzle editors.  There are more appropriate clues that didn't necessarily have to be easy.  Frankly, the clue was garbage.

@Ned S I mostly agree with you. However I did have a Honda Odyssey with what I would probably call a CD Drive. I could put audio CDs in and the data would be saved to a hard drive for future use. I think you could also buy map updates that would be loaded that way, but my memory might be faulty on that part.

@Chris

Just to clarify, ONEL is not related to a specific law school, but a designation for any first year law student (I only know that from crosswords, though.) So it wasn’t obvious but guessable if you know that term. And EVOO is extra virgin olive oil, which again I only know from crosswords.

@Chris 
The only issues I can help you with are:

EEVO apparently means Extra Virgin Olive Oil. I only know this from doing crosswords.

  and

For MOT, think of the phrase "bon MOT". MOT is the French noun for "word", and "bon" means "good", so a "bon MOT" is literally a good word, but idiomatically it means a witty remark, or quip.
However, I've never seen the isolated word MOT used as meaning "quip" (or "wit") in an actual English language sentence - only in Crosswords.
@SP I read the book by Scott Turow and assumed the term was used outside Harvard Law, but a DuckDuckGo search cannot confirm that.

@SP

I was apparently a ONE L at exactly the same time as Justice Kagan…but very quickly learned that it wasn’t for me. Although my grades were ok, after one term, I realized that it held no interest for me and I was one and done. I applied to law school because my wife had started the year before, and she loved it, but it just wasn’t for me. 

At no time during those five months did I realize I was a ONE L. Apparently, the term existed at some law schools at the time, but only became widespread later. Maybe because of “The Paper Chase.”

As a result of my experience, I’m a big believer in cutting your losses if you get involved in something that you soon find out was a mistake.

@sotto voce Oh, duh! I was stumped on this one after getting it filled in from other words in the grid. Maybe I just needed more coffee, but I had lots of those in this puzzle - filled it in correctly but had no idea what it meant...

@Grumpy
I think EVOO for extra virgin olive oil was popularized by Rachel Ray on her cooking show.

@Francis

In Brazil, there's an expression for that : I swam and swam, only to die at the beach. ;-)
@Francis Fat is a macronutrient (as are carbohydrates and protein).

@Francis

I certainly raised my eyes at that too, but since I already had BUTTS in it didn’t  trip me up.

@Jin

Yes, your body definitely needs fats. I think lots of foods have some fats in them, and you don't even know it. Like wheat germ!

@sotto voce

You want to hear my deepest, darkest secret?  Promise to keep it between us?

I would love to try LSD.  I absolutely love depictions of trips in TV and in the movies, but I've never had the guts to actually do it.  I don't even know how I'd go about it.

Also I am very risk averse, so I would need almost laboratory conditions to feel comfortable.  Other than getting into a study of how LSD affects the "quite aged" group, I doubt that I'll ever get the chance.

But, remember, mum's the word.

@Francis

You can count on me to keep a secret. I do it with my own name!

You're braver than I am. The hard stuff like that scares the living daylights out of me – probably because in my youth I had two dreams, some nights apart. 
In the first one, I had a joint. In the dream I felt exactly as I had IRL when I tried it.
In the second one, I was given something, I know not what. I had such a terrible trip, horrendous, I could hardly wake up from the dream.

I thus put two and two together and decided that I would never go any farther than the rare joint. And so it's been. 

Not even a laboratory scenario could convince me otherwise.

@sotto voce

I should further explain...I didn't feel this way 40 years ago, or even 20 years ago.  Old age has made me more adventurous is some ways.

@Francis

I've never had a bad experience on LSD, but it does last a long time. If you're psychedelic curious, I recommend starting with psilocybin. Mushrooms can be intense, but the trip doesn't last as long as LSD. And you can start small (1g) and work up from there. Also probably easier to find; LSD is pretty hard to come by.

@sotto voce

Since Francis designated this 
a private conversation,

I went to Bennington in the ‘80s.
LSD was part of the curriculum.

Paper tabs (squares), strips,
or even gel pops 
(think hardened jello, thin as paper,
that dissolves on the tongue).

I cannot deny I had some mind-blowing trips,
some euphoric,
and some terrifying.

I preferred shrooms.

But then adulting began.
Green tea, Pinot Grigio, and green smoothies
are all the buzz this chickadee can handle.

@Francis
The windowpane acid back in the 60's was borderline dangerous enough. Although psychedelic in its content, it had very a high concentrate of speed and never metabolized particularly well.  

What passes for LSD today is probably deadly. Even today's weed is questionable, unless you absolutely know you're getting indica, and *not* sativa. And it comes from a very reputable source.

Go back over your chemistry notes and tell me I'm wrong about the LSD.

@Francis


"If the doors of perception were cleansed every thing would appear to man as it is, Infinite." -William Blake

You do not need drugs, and in my humble opinion they only complicate matters. You already have everything you want in your normal everyday consciousness.

As for studies in laboratory conditions, ever hear of MKUltra?

@CCNY

And macaroons, maybe. Oh, and don't forget donuts. And ice cream cones, of course.

@Francis Well, if you wouldn't mind located to my and Dr. Goldstein's neighborhood for a while, and are OK with psilocybin instead of LSD: Investigating the Persisting Effects of a Single Dose of Psilocybin on Structural Plasticity in Healthy Older Adults https://clinicaltrials.gov/study/NCT06367738?locStr=Berkeley,%20CA&country=United%20States&state=California&city=Berkeley&intr=Psilocybin&rank=1 Unfortunately, I was disqualified for having some titanium coils in my head; it's an MRI scanning study.
@sotto voce Yes, as soon as I see her name on a puzzle, I know I'm in good hands, thank you Rebecca!

@Elbridge Gerry

TERABITS are theoretically a thing, but I agree that they’re not used. Just like centigrams and decimeters are things—things with no practical use in the real world. 

But “-bit” after a multiplicative prefix is often seen in crosswords, so we just fill it in an move on. 

The rule is, if it exists, it’s fair, not if it’s common. As long as the crosses are gentle.

@Steve "des" is French for "from"

@Steve
A little tricky, as it would be DES only for a plural noun. Like je viens des États-Unis or je viens des Pays-Bas. Otherwise it'd be "du" or "de la", or maybe just "de", depending.

@Kristin J Thank you! I was stumped by this one too. I got it filled from other clues, but I don't know any French, so I couldn't figure out how it made any sense. The X-Phile,
Not sure about wisdom, but I can share information. More than once or twice a year recently there have been Sunday clue changes after The Magazine went to bed on Wednesday. In the case of today's 1 Across, I think a really great clue in print was watered down for the digital solvers. As for your nit, I gave CD DRIVE (as clued) the side eye, but I actually found examples of it -- as clued -- online. That suggests either the clue is fine or the internet is illiterate. N.B. The CUE clue is not a debut.

@The X-Phile Agreed 100% on CD DRIVE. I KNEW that was wrong so I didn't fill it in for a while, lol. Shortz missed it somehow, or may have changed it incorrectly -- it doesn't seem like the kind of clue Rebecca Goldstein would submit, she is so consistent and precise!
The X-Phile, By the way, I had a nit about one of the themers, which I noted here early yesterday evening: https://www.nytimes.com/shared/comment/4flpkn?rsrc=cshare&smid=url-share

@The X-Phile

CD DRIVE bugged me too. Like, its technically correct but I've never heard it referred to as a DRIVE except in regards to computers. For cars I've only ever heard it referred to as a CD player.

@The X-Phile
I wasn’t keen on CDDRIVE, but it did make the clue a bit harder. I thought of a few things that we don’t see in cars anymore and then waited for some crosses. Outmoded item on a computer would have been a total gimme. Floppy drive being too long. So then we would have complained about the clue being too easy. Plus drive is technically correct, just not in the language.

@The X-Phile  - I don't think anyone in a car would say "stick that CD into the CCdrive" they would say "Stick the CD into the CDplayer." 

Nevertheless, the part of the CD player into which you would stick the CD is the CD drive. The clue didn't specify "how you would say it while driving."

@Jerry

Click on that Edit and you should see your name and location shown in separate boxes, along with your icon in a separate circle centered above with EDIT just below.(Note CAPS: this is not the same 'Edit' as before.) Click  on this 2nd EDIT  and you will be offered the chance to change your icon.

commentBody:
@Jerry
Aha, I think I see the problem. It seems to withhold the simplest possibility: the initial. Perhaps save a J as a GIF and then you  can access it.

@SBK in TO
Thank you!

@SBK in TO
Wow, I tried that a while back with no success. The letter next to everyone's name isn't an image, per se, it's text. Therefore cannot be saved.
I fixed it, as you suggested, by capturing a screenshot of the letter in the "Me" account settings on the app, cropped it, and saved it.
Voila!
Merci

@abelsey

The New York Times has written that the Sunday puzzles are supposed to be "mid-week" difficulty! They are just bigger.
@lucky13 this was Monday difficulty

Darren,
The editors have told us Sunday fill clues are supposed to be Tuesday-Wednesday level difficult. "Easy" comments appear on Sunday when the fill clues seem Monday-level or the theme does not seem the least bit challenging.

(I agree with you: this was a very nice puzzle.)

@Barry Ancona From Deb Amien's article, "How to Solve the New York Times Crossword Puzzle," November 20, 2017: "Contrary to popular belief, the Sunday puzzles are midweek difficulty, not the hardest. They’re just bigger." https://www.nytimes.com/article/how-to-solve-a-crossword-puzzle.html

lucky13,
That's Amlen, no Amien.

@Barry Ancona
My mind sees it as Ambien.

@Nan C.

Do you frequently seek things that annoy you on several levels?

unless you're on the bonnie bonnie banks of loch lomand... ;)

Well taken. I revise: A four-letter loch is almost always NESS. In NYT crossword land, that is.

shortly before he passed he put all of his songs in the public domain. class act to the end.

@Don H
I sang in the DC gay mens chorus for a while; after a concert a woman came up to us gushing how much she had enjoyed the concert. Her husband liked to sing, and she asked if one had to be gay to join. Our conductor said "Of course not.... But it probably helps."

Nancy,
I loved [Screen writing?] for EDIT too, but only those of us who solved in The Magazine saw that clue for 1 Across. Digital solvers got a Monday-level clue. I guess that on reflection the puzzle editors thought only editors would get it.

@Vaer

I'm bummed they'd give us an easier clue. Why on earth?  I agree that it was very obvious. Also agree about that corner. I finished well faster than normal.
@Barry Ancona I didn’t realize the paper and app have different clues.  That’s disappointing…

@Barry Ancona --  If the "Screen writing" clue was Rebecca's, then I consider changing it to a Monday-level clue to be a sacrilege, a betrayal, and absolutely unforgiveable.  There is no reason whatever for a constructor to strain every last brain cell to come up with a truly clever and original clue if the NYTXW is going to dumb it down.  As I've said before, I'm pretty sure that's how they lost Robyn.  For shame, Times editors!!

@Barry Ancona, I find that insulting and offensive! All puzzlers should get the same experience, at least have the same clues! Grrrr.

I started solving online when my delivery of the physical paper became unreliable. In my dinky south-of-Boston town, I was lucky to receive my delivery one weekend a month. My complaints netted me refunds on the subscription, but I wanted the dang paper. By the time I realized I didn't receive one, most local outlets had sold out. 🤷🏼‍♀️😒

Last Sunday they had different grids, 
but that's another matter!

@Cross eyed
they don't as a rule, but since the print version goes to bed early (Wednesday?) sometimes the editors decide to edit before the digital version drops--either because they've found an error or they want to adjust the difficulty, usually in the direction of easier. :\

There are 100 US SENators.
@Seward Parker thanks!  Clearly need more caffeine this morning
@Deb K- Put this on in your solving toolbox. It is fairly often clued like this or very similarly.
@Deb K There were also 100 Japanese sen in a yen before inflation reduced the yen's value. I guess both answers work, I didn't even think of the "senator" answer!
@Captain Quahog it is, and yet I almost never think of it; I nearly always find myself backing out "pct" when a cross doesnt fit.

Deb K,
You did see "Abbr." in the clue?
@Grant Agree. A CD-drive is an auxiliary device for a computer. Cars had CD-players, sometimes called CD-changers. Another stretch.

You're in your Sunday best? 
I guess this puzzle was WELL-SUITED for you.
